start.speaker: thornwood_spirit
start.text: You return to the shrine. The roots remember. Do you come with questions, or with answers?

options.text: Tell me about the original pact. Before it became sacrifice.
What does the forest truly want?
What are you, Warden? Were you always this?
Can the pact be changed? Renegotiated?
Your riddle — 'what is given freely that cannot be taken.' Why that question?
Old Maren speaks of the Thornwood Circle. What happened to the druids?
I've learned enough. Thank you, Warden.

original_pact.speaker: thornwood_spirit
original_pact.text: Before your stone villages, humans came to the forest's edge and spoke. They asked before cutting. They thanked after harvesting. The forest gave willingly — wood, fruit, shelter, medicine. This was the pact. No blood. No altar. Just conversation.

original_pact_2.speaker: thornwood_spirit
original_pact_2.text: Then came the lord who stopped asking. He demanded. When the forest withheld, he offered blood — thinking it was a price. The forest had never tasted blood before. It did not know what to do with it. It learned. And what it learned was hunger.

forest_desire.speaker: thornwood_spirit
forest_desire.text: To be heard. That is all. The forest is vast and ancient and desperately lonely. It watches your lives flicker like sparks and it wants — more than anything — for one of those sparks to sit still long enough to listen.

forest_desire_2.speaker: thornwood_spirit
forest_desire_2.text: The druids listened. For centuries, there was balance. Then the druids died or were driven away, and the forest was left speaking to silence. Imagine screaming into a void for thirty years. That is the Heartwood's pain.

warden_origin.speaker: thornwood_spirit
warden_origin.text: I am the forest's attention. Where it looks most closely, I appear. I was the face it showed to the druids — welcoming, curious, gentle. Now I am the face it shows to those who bring axes and fire.

warden_origin_2.speaker: thornwood_spirit
warden_origin_2.text: I remember being kind. I remember leading lost children home through the trees. Now I guard. I test. I fight. The forest made me what it needed, and it needs a wall more than it needs a door. I grieve that.

renegotiation.speaker: thornwood_spirit
renegotiation.text: Changed? Perhaps. The forest is old but not rigid. It adapts. It learned to accept blood — it can learn to accept something else. But the change must come from both sides. Humans must offer conversation, not sacrifice. And the forest must remember how to receive it.

renegotiation_2.speaker: thornwood_spirit
renegotiation_2.text: A new druid. That is what is needed. Not the old kind — they are gone. A new kind. Someone who stands between forest and village and translates. Someone who plants roots in stone and stones in soil. If such a person existed, the pact could change.

riddle_meaning.speaker: thornwood_spirit
riddle_meaning.text: Every truth the forest needs to hear is a truth that humans have forgotten. Trust, love, forgiveness — these were the currency of the old pact. They cannot be taken at sword-point. They can only be given. The pact broke when giving became taking.

riddle_meaning_2.speaker: thornwood_spirit
riddle_meaning_2.text: The riddle is not a test of knowledge. It is a test of instinct. Those who answer 'blood' reveal what they truly value. Those who answer 'trust' reveal what they truly seek. The forest listens to the answer behind the answer.

druids_fate.speaker: thornwood_spirit
druids_fate.text: The Thornwood Circle numbered twelve at its height. They were the bridge between forest and human. When the pact changed — when blood replaced words — the druids resisted. The first lord had six of them killed. The rest fled or hid.

druids_fate_2.speaker: thornwood_spirit
druids_fate_2.text: Old Maren is the last. Blind, old, sitting on a bench at the village edge. She still listens. She is the forest's last friend among your kind. When she dies, the bridge is ash. Unless someone rebuilds it.

warden_farewell.speaker: thornwood_spirit
warden_farewell.text: Go. The roots will remember your questions. That is more than most humans leave behind. Questions are seeds. Given time, they grow into understanding.
